title: Wall accumulation of confined active Janus colloids due to effective active diffusivity
authors: Sandeep Ramteke, Alicia Boymelgreen, Jarrod Schiffbauer
abstract: Electrokinetically-driven Janus colloids, e.g., with one metallic and one dielectric hemisphere, confined between parallel walls exhibit a boundary-accumulation mechanism enabled by an effective cross-channel diffusivity which is distinct from wall accumulation of active Brownian or run-andtumble particles. Using density-matched suspensions and three-dimensional confocal imaging, we directly measure the full time-dependent redistribution of particles across the channel under an applied AC electric field. The wall population grows exponentially while the bulk depletes, and data obtained over multiple field strengths collapse onto a single curve when rescaled by the measured relaxation rate, revealing one dominant, confinement-controlled timescale. Propulsion follows the expected induced-charge electrophoretic scaling, with a mean orientation angle lying between 2 degrees and 10 degrees above horizontal, leading to a top-biased accumulation. Comparison with an overdamped Ornstein-Uhlenbeck turning model suggests that persistent stochastic turning about a small out-ofplane angle results in a cross-channel effective drift and diffusion. The drift governs the dominant timescale and the diffusion is strong enough to provide significant accumulation on the bottom wall despite a mean upward orientational bias.
summary: This paper investigates how electrokinetically-driven Janus particles accumulate at the walls of a microfluidic channel. It demonstrates that this accumulation is not due to standard active Brownian motion but is instead driven by an effective cross-channel diffusion caused by persistent stochastic turning. The findings show that the accumulation follows a predictable exponential growth pattern governed by a single relaxation timescale.
main_problem: Understanding the mechanism behind the time-dependent wall accumulation of electrokinetically-driven Janus colloids in confined geometries and distinguishing it from standard Active Brownian or Run-and-Tumble particle models.
main_result: The study reveals that accumulation is driven by an effective cross-channel diffusivity and drift arising from persistent stochastic turning, leading to exponential wall population growth and a single confinement-controlled timescale.
method: The researchers used 3D confocal imaging of density-matched suspensions and compared experimental results to an overdamped Ornstein-Uhlenbeck turning model using a Fokker-Planck approach.
model_or_system: The system consists of 5 micrometer diameter gold-polystyrene Janus particles confined between parallel walls under an applied AC electric field, modeled via a 1D effective drift-diffusion equation derived from Langevin equations.
key_observables: Particle density distribution across z-layers, accumulation rate constant (k), propulsion velocity (Ux, Uz), and mean orientation angle (beta).
important_parameters: Electric field strength (E), voltage (V), angular relaxation rate (lambda), and effective diffusivity (Deff).
main_assumptions: Small angle approximation (beta << 1), time-scale separation (t >> tau_or), and the overdamped limit (low Reynolds number).
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the experimental setup and particle orientation; Fig 2 displays progressive particle accumulation in different z-layers; Fig 4 compares experimental depletion time with theoretical model predictions.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the experimental setup and system, describes the 3D imaging methodology, presents the theoretical derivation of the effective drift-diffusion model, compares experimental accumulation rates to the model, and discusses scaling laws and orientation dynamics.
why_interesting: The study explores non-equilibrium dynamics and effective transport coefficients emerging from stochastic angular fluctuations, which is highly relevant to understanding driven-dissipative systems and coarse-grained dynamics in open quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
